title: Human–Agent Collaboration in 2026
author: Your Name · April 2026

2026';position:absolute;bottom:10px;right:18px;font-size:.45em;color:rgba(0,212,255,.2);letter-spacing:.18em;pointer-events:none} .reveal .progress span{background:var(--neon)!important} .reveal .controls{color:var(--neon)!important}

slides.title: Human–Agent Collaboration in 2026
slides.subtitle: Designing Systems Where Developers and Agents Work Together

slides.title: Why Collaboration Is the Real Bottleneck
bullets: Agents are capable — aligning them with humans is the hard part
Core issues: trust gaps, unpredictability, insufficient control
2026 shift: from automation-first to collaboration-first design
Poor human–agent fit → rejected outputs, abandoned workflows
Ask: do users actually trust your agent's decisions?

slides.title: Modes of Human–Agent Interaction
left: Human-in-the-loop: approve before each execution
Human-on-the-loop: monitor + override in flight
Human-out-of-the-loop: full autonomy pipeline
Autonomy level must be runtime-configurable
All three modes should coexist in one system
right: HITL: high-stakes, irreversible, low-frequency ops
HOTL: bulk ops, audit trail required
HOOTL: well-tested, low-stakes, high-frequency ops
Let users escalate or de-escalate control themselves
Default to lower autonomy until trust is established

slides.title: Designing Control Surfaces for Agents
bullets: Expose per-step controls: retry, edit, approve, rollback
Surface intermediate reasoning — not just the final output
Support step-by-step execution mode for sensitive workflows
UX quality drives agent adoption more than model quality
Principle: every agent decision must be inspectable

slides.title: Feedback Loops That Actually Improve Systems
left: Explicit: thumbs up/down, inline edits, corrections
Implicit: ignored outputs, overrides, retry rates
Both types reveal what your agent is getting wrong
Store as structured events — not unstructured logs
Tag feedback to workflow step, not just the run
right: Override rate >20%? Revisit the action policy
Edit distance on outputs is a direct quality metric
Pipeline: feedback → eval set → regression tests
Route signals to: prompts, tools, workflow configs
Better feedback loops beat better prompts alone

slides.title: Prompting vs System Design
left: Brittle: breaks on distribution shift
Monolithic: one giant, hard-to-debug context blob
Unversioned: difficult to A/B test or roll back
Unconstrained: agents can go off-script easily
Opaque: hard to isolate root cause of failures
right: Prompts as versioned, swappable pipeline modules
Structured workflows with explicit step constraints
Validation and parsing layers between agent steps
Rules + LLM + output validators in sequence
Treat prompts like code: test, review, version

slides.title: Collaboration in Developer Workflows
bullets: Code gen agents: suggest diffs, humans review and merge
PR assistants: summaries + review notes, humans approve
Debugging agents: surface candidates, developer decides
CI/CD agents: flag anomalies and hold, not auto-deploy
Result: 2–3× faster iteration with humans still in control

slides.title: Handling Trust & Transparency
left: Black-box decisions with no explanation or reasoning
Inconsistent behavior across similar inputs
Silent failures or unexpected side effects
No audit trail for agent actions taken
No way to reproduce or verify what happened
right: Show: reasoning summary, tools used, sources cited
Log every action with inputs, outputs, timestamps
Make all agent runs fully reproducible on demand
Surface confidence signals directly in the UI
Trust is a feature you ship — not a side-effect

slides.title: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
bullets: Full autonomy in workflows with irreversible side effects
No visibility into intermediate steps or agent reasoning
Single monolithic agent replacing an entire workflow
Treating user corrections as noise, not feedback signals
Result: fragile systems and rapidly eroded user trust

slides.title: Future of Human–Agent Systems
bullets: Collaborative multi-agent + human teams on shared task graphs
Agents that adapt behavior to individual user patterns
Interfaces: away from chat, toward workflow-centric UIs
Developers evolve from coders to agent system designers
Best systems amplify humans — they don't replace them
